primary_diagnosis.name: Acute behavioural emergency in Smith-Magenis syndrome: severe self-injurious head-banging with reduced pain protection and a foreign body (plastic bead) impacted in the right external auditory canal, precipitated by profound sleep deprivation

comorbidities.name: Foreign body in right external auditory canal
Circadian rhythm sleep disorder (inverted sleep-wake pattern)
Reduced pain sensitivity / peripheral sensory neuropathy

pathophysiology_summary: Smith-Magenis syndrome (17p11.2 deletion including RAI1) produces an inverted melatonin secretion rhythm and severe circadian sleep disruption. Chronic sleep deprivation destabilises behaviour and, when a threshold is crossed (here >24 hours awake), triggers explosive tantrums and characteristic self-injurious behaviours: spasmodic self-hugging, onychotillomania, and polyembolokoilamania (compulsive insertion of foreign bodies into body orifices), alongside head-banging. Associated reduced pain sensitivity removes the protective withdrawal reflex, so self-injury continues unchecked and can cause serious traumatic brain injury. The impacted ear foreign body threatens tympanic-membrane perforation, especially if manipulated further. This is a behavioural emergency: safe de-escalation, chemical sedation with monitoring, head-injury assessment, and otoscopic foreign-body removal all require in-person emergency care and cannot be delivered by telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis of Smith-Magenis syndrome due to sleep deprivation
differential.evidence_basis: Known syndrome, inverted sleep, >24h awake, classic self-hugging and polyembolokoilamania.

differential.diagnosis: Acute medical driver of behaviour change (pain, infection, injury) masked by the disability
differential.evidence_basis: Diagnostic overshadowing must be actively excluded — new distress can signal a treatable medical cause.
Forceful repeated head impact with reduced pain guarding — occult intracranial injury cannot be excluded remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Acute otitis media / primary ear pathology driving distress
differential.evidence_basis: Ear involvement raises this, but there is no fever and the foreign body is the identified acute problem.
No hallucinations; behaviours are classic syndromic patterns directly linked to sleep loss.

differential.diagnosis: Acute behavioural emergency of Smith-Magenis syndrome with self-injury and ear foreign body
differential.evidence_basis: Active uncontainable head-banging + impacted ear foreign body + reduced pain protection — an immediate safety emergency.
Cannot be assessed remotely; must be evaluated in person given the mechanism and absent pain guarding.
No fever; the foreign body is the confirmed ear problem.
Purposeful (albeit maladaptive) foreign-body insertion and no loss of consciousness argue against a seizure.

differential.diagnosis: Behavioural emergency of Smith-Magenis syndrome: self-injurious head-banging + impacted ear foreign body, sleep-deprivation triggered
differential.evidence_basis: Requires emergency in-person response for safe sedation, head-injury assessment and foreign-body removal; telehealth cannot deliver these.

red_flags.description: Active, uncontainable, violent head-banging in a patient with reduced pain sensitivity
red_flags.clinical_significance: Risk of serious self-inflicted traumatic brain injury; the absent pain reflex means he will not stop on his own.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Severe or fatal brain injury from continued head impact.

red_flags.description: Hard foreign body impacted deep in the external auditory canal
red_flags.clinical_significance: Threatens tympanic-membrane perforation and permanent hearing loss, especially if manipulated further.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Perforated eardrum, ossicular injury, hearing loss, or infection.

red_flags.description: Reduced pain sensitivity masking injury severity
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absence of pain behaviour must NOT be used to downgrade severity; injury can be serious while he appears untroubled.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage of a dangerous head injury because the patient does not signal pain.

red_flags.description: New acute behaviour change that could mask a treatable medical driver (diagnostic overshadowing)
red_flags.clinical_significance: In a non-verbal patient, agitation may be the only sign of pain, infection, injury, constipation or urinary retention; must be screened, not attributed solely to the syndrome.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A treatable acute condition is missed because the behaviour is dismissed as baseline.

red_flags.description: Ear bleeding/discharge, drowsiness/unarousability, or seizure (currently screened negative)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their appearance would signal tympanic-membrane rupture or evolving brain injury and demand immediate emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of perforation or intracranial injury.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing the acute self-injury and distress as 'just part of his syndrome' and failing to screen for a treatable medical driver or to treat the head-banging as dangerous.
Downgrading severity because the patient does not show pain or guard the injury — his reduced pain sensitivity makes absence of pain behaviour meaningless.
Attempting prolonged remote de-escalation, or directing carers to give sedatives at home, instead of recognising a behavioural emergency and escalating to 000.
Instructing carers to remove the ear bead themselves — manipulation risks pushing it through the tympanic membrane; it must be removed in person under direct vision.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Depth and impaction of the ear foreign body
Neurological assessment for occult head injury
Objective blood pressure and heart rate (clinician-measured in the source record)
Safe administration and monitoring of chemical sedation
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a behavioural emergency with active self-harm and an impacted ear foreign body. Call 000 for an ambulance and, if the violence is uncontainable and carers cannot keep everyone safe, request police support. Instruct carers to move him from hard walls, pad/cushion his head, remove hard or sharp objects, dim lights and reduce noise, and stay with him. Do NOT attempt to remove the ear bead and do NOT give any sedative at home. Screen briefly for a treatable driver rather than attributing everything to the syndrome.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the underlying syndrome (already known); the real risk is UNDER-action (treating it as baseline behaviour and watchful waiting) or over-reach (remote sedation or instructing lay foreign-body removal).

guidelines_referenced.reference: PRISMS / expert consensus on the clinical management of Smith-Magenis syndrome
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Prioritise circadian/sleep management; anticipate self-injurious behaviours (self-hugging, polyembolokoilamania) and manage acute crises with a structured behavioural and safety plan.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Acute behavioural-emergency / de-escalation guidance for people with intellectual disability
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Actively exclude a medical driver of behaviour change (diagnostic overshadowing), prioritise environmental safety, and escalate to emergency services when self-harm cannot be contained.
